data.title: 7% Dividend Yield and Double-Digit Upside: Truist Suggests 2 Dividend Stocks to Buy
data.description:
Nearly three months into 2026, with the geopolitical situation showing increasing risk and the American political situation showing increased deadlocks on Capitol Hill, it’s only natural for investors to look for ways to shore up their portfolios. One classic play – buy into high-yielding dividend stocks.

Claim 30% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Dividend stocks have plenty to offer investors right now – and always. The best of them combine high yields with reliable payment histories, giving investors a steady income stream that’s an asset whether the markets run up or down.

Wall Street’s analysts know this, and the team at Truist has been tagging some high-yield dividend payers for investor attention. Of particular interest, two of these picks also feature double-digit upside potential for the coming year. Add in the dividend yields that start at 7%, and we’re looking at solid returns that will be a boon for any investing strategy.

To get the full picture, we turned to the TipRanks database to see how these Truist picks stack up with the rest of the Street. Let’s take a closer look.

Diversified Energy Company (DEC)

The first high-yielding dividend stock we’ll look at is Diversified Energy Company, a producer, transporter, and marketer of natural gas and natural gas liquids in the Appalachian and Central regions of the US. Diversified follows a coherent strategy of acquiring existing production wells that feature long-life and low-decline profiles. The company operates these wells, along with midstream assets, through an efficient management style that aims to improve or restore asset production while reducing the cost of operations. The company also works to safely retire wells that reach the end of their productive lives.

Diversified’s upstream production leans heavily toward Appalachia, where the company has 70% of its producing assets. The remaining 30% of production comes from the Central region. These production assets are backed up by a midstream network that includes over 17,000 miles of gathering and transportation pipelines. Integrating these sets of assets – upstream and midstream – allows Diversified to create stable operating margins and reliable cash flows.

On the gas marketing side, Diversified acts in 25 markets and has 261 actively managed sales points. This network handles more than 1.2 billion bcf of natural gas every day, and puts the company among the top 25 of North American natural gas marketers.

Like many energy sector operators, Diversified pays out a regular quarterly dividend. The company last declared the payment on February 26 for a June 30 payout; the dividend was set at 29 cents per common share. At that rate, the dividend annualizes to $1.16 and gives a forward yield of 7%.

On the financial side, Diversified generated $667 million in revenue during its last reported quarter, 4Q25. For the full-year 2025, revenue came to $1.83 billion, beating the forecast by $190 million and registering an impressive 141% year-over-year increase. The company had a full-year net income of $342 million, and a 2025 adjusted free cash flow of $440 million.

Truist analyst Gabe Daoud lays out an upbeat outlook for Diversified. He says of the company, “DEC stands out as a differentiated small-cap E&P owing to the company’s PDP (proved developed producing) roll-up strategy. DEC securitizes low-decline, stable, and heavily hedged (80%+) PDP cash flowing assets providing stable cash flow to support an attractive dividend yield of ~8%, one of the highest in our coverage list… Valuation stands at 3.7x ’26e EV/EBITDA – roughly 1 turns below gas -focused E&P peers – and FCF/EV yield at 30.6%. We believe the current valuation/multiple construct of DEC could re -rate higher closer to peers due to its PDP roll-up strategy.”

Quantifying his stance here, Daoud puts a Buy rating on DEC, and he complements that with a $22 price target that indicates a one-year upside potential of 32%. The forward dividend yield brings the one-year return here to 39%.

Overall, the Street gives this stock a unanimously positive Strong Buy consensus rating, based on 6 recent positive analyst reviews. The shares are trading for $16.62 and have an average target price of $21.71; this combination gives a 31% upside potential on the one-year horizon. (See DEC stock forecast)

data.title: Oil Prices Tumbled on Iran De-Escalation Hopes – J.P. Morgan Says These 2 Oil Stocks Still Make Sense
data.description:
It’s been just over three weeks since the latest escalation in the Middle East, and oil has reacted predictably. WTI crude prices have jumped over 40% since February 26 as tensions ramped up.

Claim 30% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

But now the story might be starting to turn. Comments from President Trump hinting that the fighting could ease sooner than expected sent oil sharply lower, with WTI dropping as much as about 17% at one point during Monday’s session.

A faster path to de-escalation could ease the shock rippling through global oil markets. That outcome remains uncertain, but traders are beginning to weigh a scenario where the conflict cools rather than escalates.

In the meantime, J.P. Morgan is leaning into the opportunity. The firm sees meaningful upside in two oil stocks that could strengthen a portfolio. We took it a step further, using TipRanks data to check how those ideas stack up against the broader Street view. Let’s dive in.

Crescent Energy Company (CRGY)

First up, Crescent Energy is an oil and gas exploration and production company operating in Texas and Utah. In Texas, the company holds productive assets in the rich Permian Basin and Eagle Ford formations; in Utah, Crescent is a top-3 producer in the Uinta Basin. Crescent holds 865,000 acres in its Texas positions, and 140,000 in Utah. The company has approximately 976 MMboe in proved reserves, and is guiding toward production of 328 Mboe/d by year-end 2026.

Crescent has, in recent months, been moving to both expand and streamline its operations. In December of 2025, the company completed an important acquisition – buying Vital Energy in an all-stock merger deal that was completed on December 15. The transaction was valued at approximately $3.1 billion. Also in December, the company completed a non-core asset divestiture, selling non-operating DJ Basin assets to a private buyer for $90 million in cash. In 2025, Crescent executed more than $900 million in divestiture agreements.

Also of interest to investors, Crescent pays out a dividend. The company has been paying out its dividend since 2022, and has not missed a quarterly distribution. In the last declaration, on February 25, the company set a 12-cent payment per common share, to be paid on March 25. At the annualized rate of $0.48 per common share, the current dividend gives a forward yield of 3.8%.

Turning to the company’s results, we find that Crescent generated $865 million in total revenue during 4Q25, the last period reported. Of this, $554.3 million came from crude oil, and $182.6 million came from natural gas. Natural gas liquids and ‘other’ operations accounted for the remainder. Revenue was down just over 1% year-over-year, and it missed the forecast by $18.3 million. At the bottom line, Crescent realized non-GAAP earnings of 49 cents per share; this figure was 16 cents per share better than had been expected. Along with those results, Crescent saw a leveraged free cash flow of $856 million for 2025; of that total, $239.2 million was generated during Q4.

JPM’s Arun Jayaram, an analyst ranked among the top 3% on Wall Street, covers this stock and takes an upbeat tone on it. The 5-star analyst writes, “We have been impressed with the company’s success in consolidating the Eagle Ford and improving capital efficiency, with CRGY now the third largest oil producer in the play. With CRGY’s ~$3.1 B acquisition of VTLE, the company has levered up its balance sheet to step into the Permian, a much more competitive basin for A&D. To CRGY’s credit, the company executed ~$800 MM in asset sales by the time the VTLE deal closed, reducing PF net debt to ~$4.8 B, and CRGY plans to let VTLE’s production volumes decline, which will shallow the base decline rate and extend Permian inventory life. While near-term leverage remains elevated compared to peers, with our estimate at ~1.5x at YE26, our modeling indicates that CRGY can utilize FCF to organically delever following the increase in strip prices due to the Iran conflict. Additionally, our NAV estimates that CRGY shares trade at a discount to PDP value at current strip prices… Over the long-term, we are confident in CRGY’s ability to manage its portfolio of E&P assets to generate value for shareholders.”

Following from this, Jayaram gives CRGY an Overweight (i.e., Buy) rating, with a $19 price target that implies a 12-month upside potential of 52%. (To watch Jayaram’s track record, click here)

The 10 recent analyst reviews on this stock include 7 Buys and 3 Holds, for a Moderate Buy consensus rating. The shares are trading for $12.50, and the average target price, of $14.11, suggests a gain of 13% over the coming year. (See CRGY stock forecast)

data.title: 2 New IPO Stocks Goldman Sachs Thinks the Market Is Mispricing
data.description:
Initial public offerings (IPOs) are the lifeblood of the stock markets. They bring new stocks to attract investor dollars and offer new vistas for innovators and capitalists.

Claim 30% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Although the IPO scene has been depressed in recent years, suffering from the aftershocks of the COVID pandemic, this year is shaping up to be a good one, with plenty of companies waiting to launch their initial offerings.

In a recent note, the team at Goldman Sachs lays out the case for a strong year in IPO activity, writing, “We expect 120 IPOs totaling $160 billion will come to market in 2026. IPO activity should increase this year following just 61 deals in 2025, but the rebound will largely reflect a return to normal volumes following depressed recent levels… From a macro perspective, the backdrop remains favorable for issuance. Our GS IPO Issuance Barometer sits at 139, indicating a conducive macro environment for IPOs.”

We can follow that logic forward and look closely at a couple of Goldman’s picks among the recent IPO stocks. These are shares that the firm believes the market is mispricing. According to the TipRanks database, both stocks have Strong Buy consensus ratings on the Street – and Goldman Sachs sees a higher upside than most other analysts are predicting. Let’s give them a closer look.

Forgent Power Solutions (FPS)

The first new IPO stock on our list here, Forgent Power Solutions is a leader in the field of electrical power distribution. The company manufactures and distributes a wide range of products, mainly equipment that is used in electric distribution on the power grids, in energy-intensive industries – and in the data centers that AI tech depends on.

Forgent boasts that it offers its customers multiple advantages, including a broad product portfolio capable of delivering power to technically demanding applications; a high level of manufacturing expertise, based on advanced machine tooling and dedicated production lines; and providing market-focused solutions to solve customer problems in the real world.

Supporting this vision, Forgent has 10 product manufacturing facilities, with more than 2 million square feet of floor space. The company employs over 1,800 people and operates in 5 geographic locations. Forgent’s customers can depend on end-to-end delivery, no matter what the project.

Forgent’s stock went on the market on February 5. The IPO saw ‘certain existing shareholders’ put a total of 39,413,573 shares on the market, while Forgent itself offered 16,586,427 shares. The initial price was set at $27, and the IPO raised approximately $1.5 billion. Forgent did not rake in the proceeds from shares sold by selling stakeholders; it did raise $447.8 million from its own sale of stock. Currently, the company has a market cap of more than $10 billion.

Earlier this month, on March 16, Forgent issued its first earnings release as a public company, covering fiscal 2Q26. In that report, the company reported revenue of $296 million, up 69% year-over-year and beating the forecast by $9.85 million. The company reported a $1.5 billion work backlog, up 100% year-over-year.

For Goldman’s Joe Ritchie, there’s plenty to like about this new stock. Ritchie writes of Forgent, “FPS currently sits at the epicenter of a wave of investment into electrical infrastructure driven by the proliferation of artificial intelligence, the modernization and expansion of the power grid, and industrial reshoring… Forgent is one of a few companies that can deliver (most products manufactured internally) the entire electrical powertrain for a data center, a capability that is critical for customers who prioritize speed, reliability, and single-source accountability. Forgent is also a key enabler of the ongoing expansion and modernization of the power grid, a market that accounted for 23% of FY2025 revenue… Relative to peers, we believe FPS’ sales and EBITDA growth will significantly outpace the group in the coming years driven by faster top-line growth and margin expansion.”

The 5-star analyst puts a Buy rating on these shares, backed by a $49 price target that points to a 46.5% upside potential for the coming year. (To watch Ritchie’s track record, click here)

Overall, Forgent’s Strong Buy consensus rating is based on 10 recent analyst reviews that include 9 to Buy and 1 to Hold. The shares are priced at $33.45 and the $43 average target price indicates a 28.5% gain lying in wait for the year ahead. (See FPS stock forecast)

data.title: Transformational Opportunities: UBS Suggests 2 Longevity Stocks to Buy as the $8T Aging Boom Accelerates
data.description:
For years, the conversation around getting older has focused on individuals – retirement, lifestyle, healthcare. But a bigger story has been quietly unfolding in the background. The U.S. population itself is aging, and that shift is starting to ripple far beyond personal milestones.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The Chief Investment Office at UBS views this trend as a foundation for long-term portfolio positioning. It frames “longevity” as a structural theme – a growing, aging population with evolving needs, particularly in areas like healthcare.

Healthcare is set to play a central role among longevity-focused investments, while other sectors will need to adjust to changing demand patterns. The opportunity is substantial for companies and investors that recognize the broader trend.

Laying out the situation, the UBS team writes: “We estimate that the annual longevity market opportunity was USD 5.3 trillion in 2023 and will grow by an incremental USD 2.5 trillion to reach total annual revenues of around USD 8 trillion by 2030… Our longevity portfolio theme is currently dominated by the health care sector, with the companies we expect to drive the longevity curve inflection carrying the largest weights. Medical device companies that both enable greater lifespan and benefit from growth in the elderly demographic also have significant weighting in our Longevity Equity List.”

Against this backdrop, UBS analysts are highlighting stocks tied to the longevity theme, targeting companies positioned to capture a share of the $8 trillion aging-driven opportunity. We turned to the TipRanks database to examine two of their selections and the thinking behind them.

Abbott Laboratories (ABT)

The first company we’ll look at, Abbott Laboratories, was founded in 1888 – and has built itself into a major player in the realm of medical devices and other health care products. The company has a diverse line of products, in such areas as pain and movement; diagnostic testing; cardiovascular health; nutrition; and diabetes. All of these fields – and more that Abbott Labs works in – have clear applications to the larger field of elder care.

Two fields in particular draw attention when we look at Abbott. The company’s FreeStyle Libre product is a leader in the continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) market, a vital technology in controlling and managing diabetes. Abbott’s product boasts that it is the leading CGM brand in the US. Abbott’s cardiovascular products include devices to manage such conditions as heart failure, irregular heartbeat, and mitral regurgitation.

Diabetes and cardiovascular disease are two of the leading causes of death, and Abbott’s work has put smart, effective treatments on the market. The company is also known for such products as concussion assessment devices, important in the treatment of mild traumatic brain injuries, and its line of Ensure nutrition products. Like the company’s diabetes and cardiovascular products, the Ensure nutrition supplements are widely used in elder care.

Turning to the company’s financial results, we find that Abbott’s last report, covering 4Q25, showed weak top-line results, and the shares fell sharply after the release. Total sales came to $11.46 billion, and were up 4.4% year-over-year, but the figure missed the forecast by $342.9 million. Abbott’s Medical Devices segment was the strongest sales driver, generating $5.675 billion in sales, for a 12.3% year-over-year gain. The nutrition segment led the losses, falling 8.9% from 4Q24 to land at $1.94 billion.

At the bottom line, however, Abbott Labs’ $1.50 non-GAAP EPS was up almost 12% from the prior-year period, and beat the estimates by a penny.

While the market has punished the company for the weak topline result, UBS analyst Danielle Antalffy expects sales to pick up. She writes, “We expect ABT’s growth to continue accelerating over the next few quarters and believe ABT is well positioned to benefit from an aging population. ABT’s leading position in the CGM and cardiovascular markets enable it to both drive better health outcomes and benefit from an aging population. In addition, ABT’s Nutritionals business should see demand increase from an aging population. We think an acceleration in growth combined with a potential resolution to ABT’s infant formula litigation will drive a higher valuation.”

Quantifying her stance, Antalffy put a Buy rating on ABT, along with a $158 price target that implies a 47% gain in the coming months. (To watch Antalffy’s track record, click here)

Wall Street clearly agrees that this stock has potential. ABT has a Strong Buy consensus rating, based on 15 recent reviews that show a lopsided 14 to 1 split in favor of Buy over Hold. The stock’s $107.19 trading price and $137.14 average target price together indicate a 28% one-year upside potential. (See ABT stock forecast)

data.title: Targeting 10% Dividend Yield? Analysts Name 2 High Dividend Stocks to Buy
data.description:
US stocks are currently under pressure, while oil prices continue to rise. In a way, these are expected developments: the war in the Middle East is expanding, Iran is effectively blocking the Strait of Hormuz, and several world powers have been unwilling to deploy naval assets to escort oil shipments. It would be surprising if the stock market just took it all in its stride.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

In a situation like this, investors will naturally think of defensive positions. And that’s where the high-yield dividend stocks come into play. These are shares that provide a reliable income stream, an invaluable asset when market conditions are unstable or uncertain. And when those dividends start bringing yields on the order of 10%, beating inflation by a wide margin, the benefits are clear.

Wall Street’s analysts frequently agree – and they are targeting high-yield dividend payers. We’ve used the data platform at TipRanks to look up 2 high-yield payers; here is a closer look at dividend stocks that the analysts recommend we buy.

Sixth Street Specialty Lending, Inc. (TSLX)

We’ll start with a business development company, a BDC. These companies exist to bring capital access to the small- and mid-size enterprises that make up an important and vital part of the US economy. Sixth Street Specialty Lending is a typical firm in this niche, operating to meet a real need: the need for capital and credit access in a business niche that does not always get such access through the traditional banking sector.

Sixth Street itself is a small-cap company; it has a market cap of $1.7 billion. Between its start of operations in 2011 and the end of 2025, it has originated $53.3 billion in aggregate principal amount of investments. The company follows a strict profile in choosing its own investment targets; Sixth Street’s portfolio includes companies with enterprise values between $50 million and $1 billion; EBITDA figures between $10 million and $250 million. Loan transaction sizes stand between $15 million and $350 million. Sixth Street’s investment types are diversified and include first and second lien senior secured loans, as well as unitranche loans, mezzanine debt, non-control structured equity, and common equity.

Following that profile, Sixth Street has built a portfolio that contains 143 companies and a listed fair value of approximately $3.345 billion. The company derives its own income from these investments and prioritizes a return of capital to investors. This can be seen in the company’s dividend.

Sixth Street last declared its dividend on February 12 for a March 31 payout. The dividend included a 46-cent regular common share payment and a 1-cent quarterly variable supplemental for payment on March 20. The regular dividend alone annualizes to $1.84 and gives a forward yield of 10%.

The company’s dividend is supported by its income. In Q4 of last year, Sixth Street reported $108.2 million in investment income, beating the forecast by $2.04 million. The bottom line, reported as a net investment income per share of $0.53, was more than enough to fully cover the regular dividend payment.

This stock has caught the attention of Lucid Capital Markets analyst Ethan Kaye, who lays out a clear case for the company to chart a sound path forward. Kaye writes of Sixth Street, “The company operates one of the more differentiated models in the BDC sector, leveraging the scale, sector expertise, and cross-platform capabilities of the broader Sixth Street ecosystem to pursue complex, often proprietary lending opportunities that have historically supported above-peer spreads, strong unit economics, and a near top-of-class credit track record. While sector profitability may face pressure from spread compression and declining base rates, TSLX looks well positioned to sustain excess returns through disciplined deployment tied to its cost-of-equity framework, supplemented by incremental return levers such as greater Lane 2 and Lane 3 origination, cross-platform deal flow, and the recently established structured credit joint venture.”

Kaye quantifies his stance here with a Buy rating and a $21 price target that together imply a one-year upside potential of 15%. Add in the dividend’s forward yield, and that one-year return may reach as high as 25%. (To watch Kaye’s track record, click here.)

Wall Street as a whole agrees that Sixth Street is worth buying; the stock has a Strong Buy consensus rating based on 8 recent reviews that break down to 6 Buys and 2 Holds. The stock is selling for $18.20, and its $21.19 average target price indicates room for a 16% gain over the course of the coming year. (See TSLX stock forecast.)

data.title: ‘S&P500 Outlook Points to Recovery’: Goldman Sachs Sees Headwinds Priced in and Suggests 2 Stocks to Buy
data.description:
War in the Middle East is weighing on markets, adding pressure through elevated oil prices and broader risk concerns. At the same time, the strong upward momentum seen earlier in the year has begun to moderate, leaving investors reassessing the path forward.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The key question now is how markets will evolve in the near term and how portfolios should be positioned accordingly. Goldman Sachs strategist Ben Snider has been examining these dynamics and suggests that, while the headwinds are meaningful, much of their impact may already be reflected in current market pricing. Even better, there’s a sound chance that markets will turn back upward.

“The S&P 500 decline from its January high has continued to follow the historical average equity market path following geopolitical risk events. Our baseline expectation is that the market will eventually resume its upward climb, continuing that historical pattern. However, the outlook now embeds higher oil prices, weaker US GDP growth, and later Fed easing than we and the market previously expected. While the distribution of potential outcomes is wide, the macro headwinds in our base case outlook generally appear priced, the fundamental engine of earnings growth continues to run, and valuations — while still elevated relative to history — are less demanding than they were a few months ago,” Snider wrote.

Some of Goldman’s stock analysts are running with this thesis and suggesting stocks to buy. We’ve used the database at TipRanks to look up the details on two of these Goldman Sachs picks. Let’s give them a close look and find out what makes them compelling choices in today’s market situation.

Fidelity National Information Services (FIS)

The first Goldman pick that we’re looking at here is Fidelity National Info Services, a Florida-based company that lives firmly in the fintech realm. The company offers a wide range of digital financial services. These include transaction clearing, payment processing, regulatory compliance, lending origination – everything that keeps money moving securely across the online financial ecosystem.

FIS does all of this with a global footprint. The company operates in 61 countries and employs some 57,000 people. Overall, FIS can offer its customers more than 500 financial solutions and processes – and boasts that it can handle as many as 34 billion transactions worldwide.

Last year, the company made an important announcement relating to efforts to streamline its business. FIS had held a substantial stake in Worldpay – which it sold off to Global Payments for $6.6 billion. At the same time, the company also announced that it would acquire the Issuer Solutions business from Global Payments for a purchase price of $12 billion. The move was announced in April last year, and represents a strong complement to FIS’s core banking and payments business.

When we turn to the financial side, we find that FIS reported $2.81 billion in revenue for 4Q25, the last period reported. This was up 8% year-over-year and beat the forecast by $70 million. The company’s non-GAAP EPS, at $1.68, was up 20% year-over-year, but it missed the estimates by a penny. FIS finished 2025 with an adjusted free cash flow of $2.2 billion, up 18% from 2024.

Goldman’s Will Nance covers this stock, and he is upbeat – based in large part on the streamlining business and the sound cash generation. Nance writes, “Following the unwind of the Worldpay acquisition from 2019 and the acquisition of the Credit issuer processing business, TSYS, from Global Payments, FIS is now a pure play on bank software and infrastructure solutions, with the company expecting recurring revenue growth in the MSD range per annum. The asset swap with GPN also improves FIS’s earnings quality by increasing the FCF profile of the company significantly. Also, aside from turning the page on strategic capital (re)allocation, we believe the underlying business execution has improved (per latest quarterly results), with FIS delivering on its growth algorithm and returning to a more normal operating leverage cadence.”

“The final step will be the wind down of restructuring-related costs,” Nance goes on to say, and then adds, “which have weighed on GAAP free cash flow since the Worldpay acquisition, and which we expect to fade in the coming years. We see this driving improving GAAP FCF and making the stock more attractive to investors looking for GARPy compounder stories with good capital returns and defensive revenue bases.”

Overall, in line with this stance, Nance rates FIS as a Buy. His $70 price target implies a one-year upside potential of 39%. (To watch Nance’s track record, click here)

The 17 recent analyst reviews here include 11 Buys and 6 Holds, for a Moderate Buy consensus rating. The stock is selling for $50.23, and its $67.13 average target price indicates room for a 34% gain over the next 12 months. (See FIS stock forecast)

data.title: Tesla (TSLA) and Rivian (RIVN): Goldman Sachs Looks at the Physical AI Opportunity Following Investor Meetings
data.description:
The world of AI is moving at a very fast pace. Many companies are still working through what Generative AI means for them, even as the next wave of applications begins to take shape. Agentic AI takes this a step further by allowing systems to plan, use tools, and act on their own in digital environments.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The next progression from here is Physical AI, where systems move beyond software to operate in and interact with the real world.

Goldman Sachs’ Mark Delaney, an analyst ranked among the top 2% on Wall Street, recently held investor meetings with representatives from companies operating in the Physical AI ecosystem. Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) is naturally a prominent name here, given it is developing both AVs (autonomous vehicles) and robotic humanoids (Optimus).

The company said it is making solid progress on Optimus hardware, with particular focus on improving the hand and forearm, long viewed as one of the toughest engineering challenges, while prioritizing “capability, reliability, and manufacturability” to enable scaling. Optimus uses a mix of rotary and linear actuators, with many core components developed in-house to protect IP and support performance and scalability, while commoditized parts like cameras are sourced externally. AI and model training remain a “key focus,” especially enabling the robot to translate human motion into precise physical actions. Tesla plans to deploy Optimus internally first, supported by a dedicated training “academy,” before targeting external shipments as early as next year.

Separately, Tesla highlighted continued progress in FSD and robotaxi development, noting “strong progress” from its end-to-end approach but adhering to a cautious rollout of unsupervised driving for safety reasons, with expansion expected to accelerate across multiple cities and regions, including anticipated regulatory approval in Europe, starting in the Netherlands.

The 5-star analyst currently has a Neutral rating for TSLA shares, while his $403 price target implies the stock is fully valued. (To watch Delaney’s track record, click here)

New trading tool for TSLA bulls/bears

One company looking to siphon away EV market share from Tesla is Rivian (NASDAQ:RIVN). The company is also making inroads in the Physical AI realm. Rivian highlighted steady progress on its RAP1 chip, noting receipt of B0 samples – a later-stage prototype closer to production quality – and a finalized production-intent design as it advances testing across both hardware and software, with the chip set to power its Gen 3 autonomy platform in the R2 by late 2026. Management expects RAP1 to reduce BOM costs vs. merchant silicon while also giving Rivian earlier access to leading-edge chip technology, and indicated no concerns around memory supply.

Beyond vehicles, RAP1 could be useful in robotics applications, aligning with Rivian’s spinout of Mind Robotics focused on physical AI. On autonomy, the company is progressing toward point-to-point driving later this year, seeing strong adoption of its Universal Hands-Free (L2+) system, with usage rising from 8% to 15% of miles driven, and continues to target eyes-off capability with its Gen 3 stack. Rivian emphasized that training data quality and diversity matter more than sheer volume, leveraging its sensor-rich fleet to improve models, and views autonomy not only as a vehicle sales driver but also as a standalone offering through products like Autonomy+.

Delaney’s rating for RIVN is also a Neutral, backed by a $15 price target, suggesting the shares are overvalued by 5%.

As for overall Street expectations for both names, TSLA claims a Hold consensus rating, based on a mix of 13 Buys, 11 Holds and 7 Sells. At $399.25, the average target closely matches Delaney’s objective. (See Tesla stock forecast)

data.title: Archer (ACHR) and Joby Aviation (JOBY) Get Major U.S. Backing – Here’s What Canaccord Sees Next
data.description:
Last week, the US Transportation Department (DoT) selected eight pilot projects for its eVTOL Integration Pilot Program (eIPP), about 270 days after the program was established by executive order. Developed in coordination with the FAA, the initiative will create real-world testing environments for next-generation aircraft across 26 states and involve eight manufacturers.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

State partners include the Port Authority of NY/NJ, Texas, Utah, Pennsylvania, Louisiana, Florida, and North Carolina DoTs, along with the City of Albuquerque, New Mexico. Participating manufacturers include eVTOL leaders such as Archer Aviation (NYSE:ACHR) and Joby Aviation (NYSE:JOBY), among others. Some projects will span multiple states. For instance, the Port Authority of NY & NJ plans to work with Archer, Joby (and Beta Technologies and Electra Aero) on 12 operational concepts across New England and the Mid-Atlantic, including passenger routes to and from Manhattan.

Cloud giants, governments, and startups are pouring enormous sums into new data centers designed to train and run advanced AI models. If the connections between those machines are slow or congested, the entire system becomes less efficient. That is why networking hardware and optical technologies have become essential parts of the AI stack.

High-speed switches help direct traffic between servers inside massive data centers, while optical components carry data across fiber links at extremely high bandwidth. These technologies form the connective tissue that allows AI systems to operate as a single coordinated machine rather than thousands of separate computers.

In simple terms, AI is not only a computing story – it is also a connectivity story.

That theme helps explain why TD Cowen analyst Sean O’Loughlin has been paying close attention to companies operating in this layer of the ecosystem. As AI data centers grow larger and more complex, the need for faster and more efficient data movement continues to expand.

Coherent (COHR)

Coherent’s products are top-tier, and the company’s customer base – in a range of markets, including the industrial, instrumentation, networking, and AI fields – depends on it for the latest in optoelectronic and digital communications technology, among other things.

Drilling down, it’s clear that Coherent offers a diverse and adaptable product line. These include laser and optical subsystems, optical transceivers, and advanced fiber optic lines, all of which have applications in networking and industry, and are used as essential components in optical network infrastructure. This tech is the hardware that made so much of our digital world possible: the internet, cloud computing, and now AI. Without this technological foundation, much of today’s optically-based global network would simply flounder.

Coherent’s products are also used in a variety of autonomous, independent technologies. Such tech as LiDAR, sensors, and even touch screens all depend on advanced optics in their functioning, and that is exactly the field that Coherent excels at.

This narrow waterway links the oil- and gas-rich countries of the Persian Gulf to global shipping routes and is widely considered one of the most critical energy chokepoints in the world. Yet, prices reversed sharply later in the day after President Donald Trump suggested the conflict could be nearing its conclusion. Following those remarks, Brent and WTI tumbled back below $90 per barrel by late trading.

Even after that strong reversal, crude remains far above the roughly $70 per barrel range where it traded before the U.S. and Israel launched their campaign against Iran on February 28.

“We increase PTs and continue to see a positive risk/reward for Energy… The longer the Middle East conflict goes on, the more upside we see to the entire curve. We do not believe the stocks and forward curve reflect that risk, as both have moved <3% since Feb 20th, the Friday before the conflict. We also see continued upward pressure on global natural gas prices due to potential Qatar supply concerns. Based on this setup, combo oil/nat gas companies potentially have the most FCF upside,” the team wrote.

With that in mind, the UBS analysts have singled out two energy stocks they believe are particularly well positioned to benefit if oil prices remain elevated. Using the TipRanks database, we took a closer look at how these picks stack up against the broader Wall Street consensus. Let’s dive in.

Magnolia Oil & Gas (MGY)

We’ll start in Houston, Texas, where Magnolia Oil & Gas operates as an independent producer in the hydrocarbon field. The company’s footprint is in the southeastern part of the state, specifically in the areas of Giddings and Karnes; the company’s Karnes holdings are considered a ‘coveted’ location, in the core of the Eagle Ford Shale formation.

What this means is that Magnolia works in some of Texas’ richest oil and gas regions. The company had an average daily production in 4Q25 of 103.8 Mboe/d; this figure indicated that the company’s production accelerated during the year – for the full year 2025, average daily production came to 99.8 Mboe/d. The Q4 production figure was up 11% from the final quarter of 2024. Production in the Giddings area, where Magnolia has its most extensive holdings, represented 79% of the total. Like many energy producers, Magnolia pays out a reliable dividend to its investors. The company has been gradually raising that payment since 2022; the last declaration, made on January 29, was for a $0.165 payment on March 2 to common shareholders. At that rate, the dividend annualizes to 66 cents per common share and gives a forward yield of 2.3%.

Turning to the financials, in 4Q25, Magnolia reported $317.6 million in quarterly revenue. This was down 2.7% year-over-year, but it did beat the forecast by $3.89 million. The bulk of this revenue, over $215 million, came from oil production. Natural gas and natural gas liquids made up the remainder. Magnolia reported a 4Q diluted EPS of 37 cents; this was down 16% from the prior-year period, although it came in $0.01 ahead of the estimates.

UBS analyst Peyton Dorne counts several reasons why investors should get on board here. Dorne writes, “We lift our MGY PT from $29 to $35, largely reflecting our increase in our 2027E EV/EBITDAX PT multiple from 4.85x to 5.75x. This is a premium to the 5.0x 5-year average multiple, which we see as warranted due to the higher near-term oil price levels, MGY’s underlying inventory growth, and its successful track record in pairing shareholder returns with underlying volume growth. MGY remains our Top Pick amongst SMID Cap E&Ps because of its durable capital returns, balance sheet strength, and top tier capital efficiency, demonstrated by its peer leading reinvestment rate.”

The company ran a net loss, however; the EPS for 4Q25 came to ($0.89), a loss that was much deeper than the ($0.34) reported in the prior-year period and fell short of the estimates by $0.21.

Oppenheimer’s Param Singh covers this stock, and is upbeat for a number of reasons. Laying out those reasons, Singh says, “Our bullish thesis for CoreWeave is predicated on: (1) the outsized TAM opportunity for AI-optimized IaaS; (2) its strong competitive positioning against large hyperscalers/cloud vendors, and other neo-clouds; and (3) its ability to deliver sustainable high FCF margin upon reaching maturity. We believe investor concerns with near-term CapEx/debt requirements are short-sighted, and as CoreWeave exits hyper-growth, cash return should improve and re-rate the stock higher.”

Along with these comments, Singh puts an Outperform (Buy) rating on the stock – and a price target of $140 that suggests a 92% upside potential for the coming 12 months. (To watch Singh’s track record, click here)

The Moderate Buy consensus rating on this stock is based on 21 recent reviews that include 12 Buys, 8 Holds, and 1 Sell. The stock’s share price of $72.99 and average target price of $116.16 together imply a one-year gain of 59% for the stock. (See CRWV stock forecast)

data.title: Goldman Sachs Sees Market Warning Signs – But Likes These 2 Stocks
data.description:
Is everything old new again? We had a financial crisis in 2008, and while market moods are better than that these days, some analysts are starting to see some disturbing parallels.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

That’s the view of Goldman Sachs strategist Peter Oppenheimer, who notes that risk premia in the equity markets are down and are running at levels we haven’t seen since the run-up to the 2008 crisis. While that might present a presentiment of the bad old days, Oppenheimer isn’t all doom-and-gloom; in a recent note, he warns that what we’re looking at is more in line with a correction than a full-out bear market.

While risk may be increasing, the Goldman Sachs stock analysts still see shares worth buying. We’ve opened up the TipRanks data platform to look at two stocks which the firm likes, even in today’s environment. Let’s give them a closer look and find out why Goldman Sachs is recommending Buys.

American International Group (AIG)

We’ll start in the insurance and underwriting space, with AIG. This company has been around for over a century, and today its global platform is available in more than 200 countries. AIG wrote $23.9 billion in net premiums in 2024, and another $23.8 billion in 2025. The company operates through three main segments: North American Commercial, International Commercial, and Global Personal.

Among the insurance products available through AIG are property and casualty policies, accident and health policies, and financial, life, and liability coverage. AIG also has financial services such as retirement solutions and asset protection. It’s all related to risk management and future security.

AIG pays out a regular dividend to its shareholders, and its last declaration, made on February 10, set a March 30 payout of a 45-cent common share payment. At that rate, the dividend annualizes to $1.80 per common share and gives a 2.25% forward yield. Last year, AIG returned $6.8 billion worth of capital to its shareholders; this return was made through $5.8 billion in share repurchases, and $1 billion in dividend payments.

In its 4Q25 report, released in February, AIG reported $670 million in general insurance underwriting income, a total that was up 48% from the prior-year period. The bottom line reported a non-GAAP adjusted after-tax income per diluted share of $1.96, for a 51% year-over-year gain and beating the forecast by 6 cents per share.

Goldman analyst Robert Cox says of this insurance giant, “We see AIG as a company with peer-high earnings growth and an improving ROE over the coming years (similar to CB), while peers will generally face decelerating ROEs and slower earnings’ growth due to cyclical pressures. While we do not think AIG is immune by any means to cyclical pressures, AIG’s earnings growth is supported by a strong underwriting position, recent accretive deals that should increase operating and premium leverage, and capital flexibility given ongoing sell-downs of its CRBG stake.”

He goes on to add about the company’s prospects, “AIG’s 1.1x P/B (10.4x P/E) valuation is attractive relative to an ROE regression, especially considering our estimates for the trajectory of stronger fundamentals relative to peers. Furthermore, AIG appears amongst the best positioned as it relates to artificial intelligence and other headline risk (including affordability) as an underwriter focused primarily on large account, multi-national, commercial insurance underwriting, and appears to be an AI investment first-mover based on company commentary and disclosure.”

All told, these comments support Cox’s Buy rating, while his $90 price target implies a one-year upside potential of 12%. (To watch Cox’s track record, click here.)

Turning to the general Street view, this stock gets a Moderate Buy consensus rating, based on 17 recent reviews, including 5 Buys and 12 Holds. The shares are trading for $80.07 and the $87 average target price indicates a 9% gain in the year ahead. (See AIG stock forecast.)

data.title: ‘Valuation Extremely Inexpensive,’ Says Top Analyst After Broadcom (AVGO) Earnings Address Investor Concerns
data.description:
Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) is hogging the limelight in Thursday’s trading with the shares pushing higher after the chip giant delivered fiscal first-quarter results that showed the semi giant’s strong growth trajectory remains intact.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Broadcom generated revenue of $19.31 billion (up 29.4% year-over-year) and adj. EPS of $2.05, slightly ahead of Street expectations of $19.26 billion and $2.03. Semiconductor revenue came in at $12.52 billion vs. the Street’s $12.31 billion forecast, while software revenue was a touch below at $6.80 billion vs. $6.86 billion. The standout was AI-focused semiconductors, which generated around $8.4 billion, up 106% YoY and beating the Street’s $8.2 billion estimate. Gross margins came in at 77.0%, down roughly 90 basis points quarter-over-quarter but slightly above expectations, while operating expenses were lower than expected.

Looking ahead, the FQ2 guide impressed, driven by an optimistic AI outlook. Revenue is expected to reach $22.0 billion with EBITDA margins of 68% and adj. EPS of ~$2.35, well above Street estimates of $20.5 billion, 66.4%, and $2.16. Semiconductors are expected at $14.8 billion, including AI sales of approximately $10.7 billion – up $2.3 billion sequentially and $1.5 billion above Street estimates. Gross margins are expected to remain near 77%, just above consensus, and software revenue is projected at about $7.2 billion, growing modestly QoQ and roughly in line with expectations.

Bernstein’s Stacy Rasgon, an analyst who ranks among the top 2% on Wall Street, hailed a solid print, backed by a “very strong” guide, and thinks the company “knocked down (at least) four outstanding investor concerns” on the call.

First, management reassured investors worried about the previous ~$73 billion AI order outlook by signaling a clear path to nearly 10 GW of AI shipments and over $100 billion in AI revenue by FY27. Second, the company noted that gross margin dilution from AI rack sales is not expected to be significant. Third, investors have been concerned about “customer-owned tooling” and the possibility of rising competition. Management pushed back on that view, arguing the risk is limited given the company’s technological capabilities and rapid pace of innovation. Finally, management addressed investor concerns that AI could disrupt the company’s software business, a fear that has recently weighed on sentiment. They argued the opposite – that their software stack, particularly VMware Cloud Foundation, represents a critical infrastructure layer for running AI workloads.

After a strong quarter and an encouraging outlook for AI-driven growth, Broadcom’s fundamentals are looking very robust and at a decent price to boot. “It now appears that EPS approaching $20 a share could conceivably be in the realm of possibility for next year,” said the 5-star analyst, “putting valuation extremely inexpensive relative to likely earnings power, with accompanying margins and free cash flow entering rarefied territory.”

Accordingly, Rasgon maintained an Outperform (i.e., Buy) rating on the shares and raised his price target from $475 to $525, implying the stock will appreciate by 58% over the one-year timeframe. (To watch Rasgon’s track record, click here)

Most other analysts are on the same page; based on a mix of 29 Buys vs. 2 Holds, AVGO receives a Strong Buy consensus rating. Going by the $457.89 average target, 12 months from now, investors will be pocketing returns of 38%. (See AVGO stock forecast)

Such a statement might sound hyperbolic, but that claim probably won’t come as much of a surprise to Tigress Financial’s Ivan Feinseth, who has just increased his price target for NVDA from $350 to a new Street-high of $360, suggesting the shares will rise by 97% over the coming months. Naturally, Feinseth’s rating stays a Buy. (To watch Feinseth’s track record, click here)

Simply put, Nvidia is at the forefront of GPU accelerators and full systems for AI training and inference, serving hyperscalers, enterprises, and sovereign AI projects. Its Data Center business has become the major revenue driver and is still “growing rapidly.” As Blackwell “ramps into volume” for these same customers, and future architectures like Rubin/Vera Rubin and Feynman reduce inference costs and increase throughput, Feinseth says Nvidia “sustains an ongoing AI capital investment cycle that widens its moat and supports premium pricing.”

At the same time, the company’s ecosystem – including CUDA, cuDNN, and a wide range of AI frameworks and SDKs – supports millions of developers and thousands of enterprise applications, creating high switching costs and strong customer loyalty. On the enterprise side, offerings like Nvidia AI Enterprise and vertical stacks for healthcare, industrial digital twins, and robotics generate recurring software and service revenue alongside hardware sales.

Feinseth also points out that platforms like Omniverse and industrial digital twins further fuel GPU and AI demand in design, simulation, and factory optimization, linking real-world applications back to the data center. Meanwhile, Nvidia’s DRIVE and robotics platforms bring AI to the edge, and sovereign AI projects expand its reach into automotive, logistics, and national compute infrastructure, broadening the company’s total addressable market.

There’s so much money being spent on AI-related endeavors, and much of it is heading Nvidia’s way. Aggregate capital investment from leading hyperscalers and cloud providers is expected to exceed $650 billion in 2026, with Nvidia capturing a significant portion through its industry-leading GPUs. The total AI market is expected to reach $3–$4 trillion by 2030 as hyperscalers, enterprises, and sovereigns reconfigure data centers into AI “factories.”

Moreover, high-value networking and system attachments – like NVLink, NVL72, Spectrum‑X, and InfiniBand – along with hardware-software co-design and the CUDA/AI Enterprise ecosystem, create “deep platform lock-in.”

“Combined with exceptional free‑cash‑flow generation, disciplined reinvestment in R&D and capacity, and robust capital returns through buybacks and dividends, these growth engines drive rising returns on capital and expanding economic Profit, translating into accelerating long‑term shareholder value creation,” the 5-star analyst summed up.

data.title: Sell Rivian (RIVN) Shares Because Its Expectations Are Too High, Says BofA
data.description:
Rivian (NASDAQ:RIVN) investors are hoping the release of its more affordable mid-size SUV, the R2, will be a game-changer for the company, but it is unlikely to have the desired effect.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

That is the opinion of BofA analyst Alexander Perry, who believes the electric vehicle maker will find it hard to gain traction in the current environment.

Perry anticipates that EV adoption will remain relatively modest, which could limit near-term profit growth amid a challenging regulatory backdrop, including the expiration of federal tax credits and the rollback of CAFÉ and greenhouse gas standards that previously supported regulatory credit revenue. Even under a more supportive regulatory framework, Perry believes U.S. consumer uptake of EVs has lagged expectations due to concerns over affordability, driving range, charging availability, and low resale values.

Perry sees overall EV penetration falling to 6% of light vehicle sales in 2026, down from 8% in 2025, due to the phase-out of EV incentive programs and a reduced focus on EVs by traditional automakers, particularly Ford and General Motors. The analyst’s forecast implies a more than 20% drop in industry EV sales, contrasting with Rivian’s call for a 53% increase in deliveries (including the R2 model, flat if excluding it), which would suggest market share gains while competitors shift toward higher-margin internal combustion engine vehicles and scale back premium EV programs.

Perry does think the R2 launch will broaden Rivian’s addressable market, noting that roughly 69% of U.S. EVs sold in 2025 were priced under $50K, or about 845,000 units. That said, competition in this price segment is intensifying, with Tesla introducing more affordable Model 3/Y standard variants ($37K/$40K), priced 13%/11% below their original models, Lucid planning a mid-size platform under $50K in 2027 – 37% below its current Gravity Platform – and Ford aiming for more affordable EVs built on its universal platform starting in 2027.

The analyst also believes Rivian is rapidly progressing in its autonomous driving technology, though he notes it still trails leading player Tesla.

All that is to say Perry reinstated coverage of RIVN stock with an Underperform (i.e., Sell) rating, backed by a $14 price objective, implying shares will decline by 6% over the one-year timeframe. (To watch Perry’s track record, click here)

Perry says his bearish take could be invalidated should Rivian rollout additional autonomy features, which could increase deliveries – especially point-to-point capabilities later this year – and by stronger-than-expected demand for the R2 given its more affordable $45K price point.

5 other analysts join Perry in the bear camp, but with an additional 8 Buys and Holds, each, the stock claims a Hold consensus rating. Going by the $17.32 average target, a year from now, shares will be changing hands for a 16% premium. (See RIVN stock forecast)

data.title: GM and Ford: Bank of America Names 2 Top Auto Stocks to Buy for 2026
data.description:
The changeover of Presidential Administrations always brings changes of policy priorities, and those can ripple outward. Sometimes, it’s impossible to know where the ripples will end, and sometimes, it’s easy. Last month, one of the easier ripples made itself felt.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The second Trump Administration took office promising to roll back large parts of the Biden regulatory regime, including difficult findings on greenhouse gases and automobile fuel economy. The greenhouse gas standard, the GHG, is a rollback, meaning that the Feds have rescinded their designation of carbon dioxide as a ‘threat to public health and welfare.’ The fuel economy measure concerns CAFE, or corporate average fuel economy, which is used to set gas mileage and efficiency standards for cars on the road. Trump’s Administration has lowered the standard, making it easier for automakers to comply – the higher Biden standard was designed to promote construction of electric vehicles.

This new regulatory model will allow Detroit’s major automakers, Ford and GM, to lean harder into high-margin trucks and SUVs, their big money-makers, while dialing back orders for less popular electric vehicles.

Bank of America is following this conclusion to its logical end: that the Detroit legacy names are going to benefit from this chance to change course under a more favorable regulatory environment. The bank is naming General Motors (NYSE:GM) and Ford (NYSE:F) – two legendary names in the automotive industry – as top auto stocks for 2026. Let’s give them a closer look, and find out why these veterans of Detroit’s Big Three are getting Bank of America’s thumbs-up.

General Motors

We’ll start with General Motors, the owner of the Chevy, Buick, Cadillac, and GMC nameplates. These include some highly recognizable names, such as the Chevy Silverado pickup truck and the Cadillac Escalade SUV. GM has been selling industry-leading cars for over a century; the company was formed as a holding company in 1908, and its first brand acquisition was Buick. The company today boasts a $71 billion market cap, and in 2025 claimed a market share of the US automotive industry in excess of 17%.

A look at some numbers will show how strong GM’s position is. Last year, the company’s total auto sales came to 2.85 million vehicles, a figure that was up 6% year-over-year; two of GM’s full-size pickups, the Chevy Silverado and the GMC Sierra, led the American market for the sixth consecutive year, with combined sales of 940,000 units. GM is also doing well in the lower-end parts of the market, and last year sold almost 700,000 Buick and Chevy cars priced below $30,000.

It’s a known fact that electric vehicle sales have been declining in recent months – the Financial Times reports that the world’s auto industry has absorbed a $65 billion hit over the past year from paring back its EV production – but internal combustion engine vehicles are rising. GM, last month, announced a $46 million investment at an assembly plant in Ontario, Canada – a plant that builds Chevy Silverado trucks.

When we turn to the direct financial results, we find that GM finished 2025 with $45.3 billion in revenue for the fourth quarter, down 5% year-over-year. The fourth-quarter figure missed the forecast by $750 million. At the same time, earnings were sound – the non-GAAP EPS, at $2.51, was up some 30% year-over-year, and exceeded the forecast by 25 cents per share.

Looking ahead for this automaker, Bank of America’s Alexander Perry sees a strong future. The industry expert writes, “We believe GM is a key beneficiary of recent regulatory changes including the removal of CAFÉ penalties, and GHG relief, which is enabling a mix shift to its most margin accretive trucks/SUVs and away from unprofitable EVs. The evidence is compelling: we estimate that trucks/SUVs variable profit per unit is $17.5K vs. corporate average of $10-12K. We also believe GM should benefit in 2026 from lower warranty costs & regulatory credits, lean inventory levels, and low incentives.”

With these potential gains in mind, Perry rates GM as a Buy, with a $105 price target that implies a one-year upside of 34%. (To watch Perry’s track record, click here.)

GM’s shares are currently trading for $78.60; the average price target, at $95.76, suggests a gain of 22% for the year ahead. The stock’s Moderate Buy consensus rating is based on 19 recent recommendations that include 14 Buys, 4 Holds, and 1 Sell. (See GM stock forecast.)
